exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy and Reliability
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI outputs are trustworthy, accurate, and consistent with clinical reality.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI systems generating incorrect alerts or missing real pathology.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: High false positive rate in lung nodule detection
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: AI frequently flags benign opacities or vessels as nodules, leading to unnecessary follow-up CTs.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Missed subtle fractures on trauma X-rays
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: AI fails to detect non-displaced or hairline fractures that experienced radiologists recognize through pattern context.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Overconfidence in ambiguous findings
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: AI assigns high confidence scores to findings that are inherently indeterminate without clinical correlation.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Generalization Across Populations and Scanners
exp_L2_00_01.description: AI models trained on limited datasets fail in real-world diversity.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Poor performance on pediatric or geriatric anatomy
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Models trained predominantly on adult mid-life patients misinterpret age-specific normal variants.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Inconsistent results across MRI vendors
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: AI segmentation tools behave unpredictably when switching between Siemens, GE, and Philips protocols.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Unreliable on low-resource imaging
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: AI degrades significantly on portable or older-generation equipment common in rural or underserved settings.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration and Efficiency
exp_L1_01.description: How AI tools fit—or disrupt—existing clinical reading routines and reporting systems.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Alert Fatigue and Cognitive Load
exp_L2_01_00.description: Excessive or poorly timed AI notifications interfere with concentration and decision-making.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Too many overlapping bounding boxes on chest X-rays
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: AI overlays obscure underlying anatomy, forcing radiologists to toggle layers repeatedly.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Non-actionable incidental findings flagged routinely
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: AI highlights clinically irrelevant findings (e.g., small renal cysts) that don’t require intervention.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Disruption of visual search pattern
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: AI annotations break the natural eye-tracking sequence used during systematic image review.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Interoperability and PACS Integration
exp_L2_01_01.description: Technical hurdles in embedding AI into existing radiology IT infrastructure.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Lack of DICOM-compliant output from AI vendors
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Results can't be stored or retrieved within standard PACS workflows.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Inability to edit or override AI markup
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: Radiologists cannot easily correct or delete erroneous AI annotations in the viewer.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Latency during peak reading hours
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: AI processing slows down hanging protocols when multiple studies queue simultaneously.

exp_L1_02.label: Clinical Responsibility and Liability
exp_L1_02.description: Uncertainty about accountability when AI contributes to diagnostic errors.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Ambiguous Accountability
exp_L2_02_00.description: Blurred lines between human and algorithmic responsibility for missed diagnoses.

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: No clear audit trail for AI-assisted reads
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: It’s unclear whether a finding was initially detected by AI or the radiologist during legal review.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Pressure to accept AI suggestions to meet productivity targets
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: Incentive structures may encourage uncritical adoption of AI outputs.

exp_leaf_02_00_02.label: Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators
exp_leaf_02_00_02.description: Hospital leadership may cite AI output as 'ground truth' when questioning radiologist judgment.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Informed Consent and Patient Communication
exp_L2_02_01.description: Challenges explaining AI involvement to patients and obtaining appropriate consent.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Patients assume AI = infallible
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Patients often believe AI tools are more accurate than human experts, creating unrealistic expectations.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Lack of standardized disclosure language
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: No consensus on how or when to inform patients that AI was used in their diagnosis.

exp_leaf_02_01_02.label: Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians
exp_leaf_02_01_02.description: When AI and radiologist disagree, it’s hard to articulate why human interpretation prevails without sounding defensive.

exp_L1_03.label: Professional Autonomy and Expertise Erosion
exp_L1_03.description: Concerns that AI may undermine radiologists’ clinical judgment or long-term skill development.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Deskilling and Overreliance
exp_L2_03_00.description: Gradual loss of diagnostic acumen due to habitual dependence on AI assistance.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Trainees skip systematic review when AI is active
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: Residents rely on AI highlights instead of developing independent search patterns.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: Less exposure to subtle or uncommon pathologies leads to atrophy of pattern recognition skills.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: Even experienced radiologists second-guess themselves when AI disagrees, especially under time pressure.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Loss of Nuanced Interpretation
exp_L2_03_01.description: AI’s binary or probabilistic outputs oversimplify complex diagnostic reasoning.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: AI ignores clinical history integration
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: Algorithms analyze images in isolation, missing key context like recent surgery or immunosuppression.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: AI provides point estimates without expressing the spectrum of possible interpretations.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: Oversimplification of borderline cases
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: Findings that exist on a continuum (e.g., mild vs. moderate stenosis) are forced into discrete categories.

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess and build trust in AI-generated predictions from virtual cell models.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment Criteria
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts judge whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which specific quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, biological plausibility, reproducibility) influence expert judgment of virtual cell model reliability.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore how prior biological knowledge shapes experts’ acceptance or skepticism of model outputs.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Assess impact of uncertainty communication
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate whether and how communicating prediction confidence or uncertainty affects perceived reliability.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs for Causal Reasoning
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations help experts understand causal mechanisms in gene perturbation outcomes.

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Clarify need for mechanistic explanations of gene-gene effects
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Determine if experts require explanations of why perturbing one gene leads to changes in others, including pathway-level reasoning.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Identify desired granularity of explanations
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Understand whether experts prefer gene-level, pathway-level, or systems-level explanations for perturbation effects.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Evaluate utility of XAI techniques for perturbation understanding
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Assess which explainable AI methods (e.g., saliency maps, feature importance, example-based explanations) would enhance comprehension of model behavior under perturbation.

res_L1_01.label: Domain Workflow Integration
res_L1_01.description: How AI-assisted tools fit into or disrupt existing experimental and analytical workflows in genetic research.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Manual Workflow Practices
res_L2_01_00.description: Characterize the standard procedures experts follow when conducting genetic perturbation experiments without AI.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Map manual perturbation experiment workflow steps
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Document the sequence of tasks, decision points, and data sources used in traditional gene perturbation studies.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify pain points in current workflows
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Uncover bottlenecks, inefficiencies, or error-prone stages in manual experimentation that AI could address.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Determine data integration practices
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Understand how experts combine experimental results with literature, databases, or prior knowledge during analysis.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Tool Adoption Barriers & Enablers
res_L2_01_01.description: Factors that facilitate or hinder integration of AI models like CellForge into routine practice.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Assess familiarity and prior experience with AI gene models
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Gauge exposure to and usage patterns of existing AI-based gene prediction tools to contextualize adoption readiness.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Identify key problems AI should solve
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Elicit specific scientific or operational challenges experts hope AI-assisted virtual cells can resolve (e.g., hypothesis generation, reducing wet-lab costs).

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Surface concerns about model limitations
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Capture worries about false positives, lack of generalizability, or misalignment with biological reality that may impede use.

res_L1_02.label: User Understanding & Knowledge Acquisition
res_L1_02.description: What knowledge domain experts aim to gain from interacting with virtual cell models and how they learn from them.

res_L2_02_00.label: Desired Knowledge Outcomes
res_L2_02_00.description: The types of biological insights experts seek through AI-assisted perturbation simulation.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Rank the importance of goals such as identifying driver genes, understanding regulatory networks, or discovering drug targets.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Clarify interest in trait-level vs. molecular-level effects
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine whether experts care more about downstream phenotypic outcomes or intermediate gene expression changes.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Assess need for comparative perturbation analysis
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Explore whether users want to compare multiple perturbations side-by-side to infer relative impacts or synergies.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Comprehension Through External Evidence
res_L2_02_01.description: How supplementary information sources can improve understanding of AI predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify trusted external data sources for validation
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which resources (e.g., literature, pathway databases, prior experiments) experts would use to verify or contextualize model outputs.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Evaluate role of literature integration in sensemaking
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Assess whether linking predictions to relevant publications or known mechanisms would increase trust and usability.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Determine format preferences for supporting evidence
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Understand how experts prefer to consume auxiliary information—e.g., inline citations, interactive knowledge graphs, or summary panels.

res_L1_03.label: Challenges in AI Interaction
res_L1_03.description: Obstacles experts face when using or interpreting AI models in genetic research contexts.

res_L2_03_00.label: Comprehension Difficulties
res_L2_03_00.description: Specific aspects of AI predictions that are hard for experts to understand or reason about.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Characterize types of prediction misunderstandings
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Identify common confusions—e.g., mistaking correlation for causation, misinterpreting confidence scores, or overlooking model assumptions.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Assess difficulty in tracing model logic
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Determine whether experts struggle to follow how input perturbations lead to specific output predictions through the model’s internal logic.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Identify gaps in mental model formation
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Explore whether users can form accurate mental models of how the virtual cell responds to interventions over time.

res_L2_03_01.label: Tool Usability & Interaction Design Needs
res_L2_03_01.description: Interface and interaction requirements to support effective use of virtual cell models.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Elicit preferred interaction modalities for perturbation design
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Understand how experts want to specify, modify, and iterate on perturbation scenarios (e.g., drag-and-drop genes, batch uploads, natural language).

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Determine needs for result exploration and filtering
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Identify desired capabilities to explore, sort, filter, or drill down into perturbation results based on biological relevance or statistical significance.

res_leaf_03_01_02.label: Assess demand for collaborative or shareable analysis features
res_leaf_03_01_02.description: Explore whether experts need to share perturbation setups, results, or interpretations with colleagues for discussion or validation.

conflict.expert_branch: Unreliable on low-resource imaging
conflict.researcher_branch: Surface concerns about model limitations

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the real-world performance drop of AI diagnostic tools when used with low-resource imaging equipment—like portable or older scanners in rural clinics—is critically important, as it directly impacts patient outcomes in underserved settings.

pivot.limitation: However, addressing hardware-specific degradation alone may not prevent clinicians from distrusting or rejecting the tool if they perceive its decisions as biologically implausible or statistically inconsistent, even when the image quality is adequate.
pivot.research_goal: Capturing and addressing surface concerns about model limitations—such as false positives or misalignment with clinical intuition—builds the trust necessary for consistent adoption across diverse care environments.
pivot.compelling_reason: By proactively surfacing and resolving these interpretability and plausibility gaps, you ensure that your reliable low-resource solution isn’t sidelined due to avoidable skepticism, maximizing its real-world impact where it’s needed most.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a rural clinic, a nurse uses the AI tool confidently during a morning triage session, knowing its alerts align with both local imaging constraints and clinical reasoning. Later, during a team huddle, the physician references the AI’s transparent rationale to explain a subtle finding to a community health worker—turning the tool into a shared language for care, not just a black box.

aligned_path_expert: Too many overlapping bounding boxes on chest X-rays
aligned_path_researcher: Identify pain points in current workflows

conflict.expert_branch: Too many overlapping bounding boxes on chest X-rays
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify pain points in current workflows

structural_mapping.inputs: A chest X-ray image with AI-generated annotations (e.g., bounding boxes for nodules, effusions, lines)
structural_mapping.logic: Annotations interrupt the radiologist’s systematic eye-tracking path (e.g., left-to-right, top-to-bottom scan), forcing reorientation or layer toggling to recover anatomical context
structural_mapping.outputs: Increased time per case, missed findings due to broken attentional flow, and higher cognitive load

analogy.explanation: Just as the researcher seeks to identify pain points in manual workflows by observing where human operators struggle, stall, or err, the 'disruption of visual search pattern' is a specific, observable pain point in radiology workflows caused by AI interface design. Both approaches treat workflow friction—whether in genetic pipetting or image interpretation—as a signal of inefficiency that AI should alleviate, not exacerbate.

scenario.inputs: A frontal chest X-ray showing a central line, a small right upper lobe nodule, mild cardiomegaly, and trace pleural effusion; the AI system overlays four semi-transparent bounding boxes that partially overlap near the hilum, obscuring lung parenchyma and vessel contours
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s method would document this moment as a workflow bottleneck: the radiologist pauses, toggles off two boxes, scrolls back to compare prior studies, then re-enables annotations—adding 22 seconds and breaking diagnostic momentum. This data point would feed into redesigning annotation salience or interaction logic.
scenario.edge_cases: When the overlapping boxes actually correspond to a complex, clinically relevant finding (e.g., a tumor invading multiple structures), reducing visual clutter might hide critical spatial relationships—highlighting tension between simplification and clinical fidelity that the researcher’s general workflow analysis may overlook without domain-specific validation.

conflict.expert_branch: Non-actionable incidental findings flagged routinely
conflict.researcher_branch: Surface concerns about model limitations

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on reducing alert fatigue by minimizing routine flags for non-actionable incidental findings—like benign renal cysts—is critically important for preserving clinician attention and workflow efficiency in real-world diagnostic settings.

pivot.limitation: However, without systematically evaluating whether the AI’s notion of 'non-actionable' aligns with biological validity and clinical context across diverse patient populations, we risk automating biases or missing subtle signals that could become relevant under different conditions.
pivot.research_goal: Surface concerns about model limitations—such as false positives rooted in poor generalizability or misalignment with underlying biology—to ensure flagged findings are not just clinically irrelevant today, but reliably so across contexts.
pivot.compelling_reason: This safeguards your goal of reducing cognitive load by ensuring that de-prioritized findings are truly safe to ignore, preventing future rework, liability, or erosion of trust when edge cases reveal hidden relevance.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, radiologists review AI-assisted scans with confidence: the system only surfaces findings validated against both clinical actionability and biological plausibility. They spend less time dismissing noise and more time on complex cases, knowing the AI’s silence on incidental findings reflects robust, trustworthy judgment—not just convenience.

conflict.expert_branch: Disruption of visual search pattern
conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize types of prediction misunderstandings

attributes: Spatial proximity of AI cues to regions of interest
Temporal sequence of annotation appearance during review
Expert’s familiarity with the image type or modality
Density of true-positive findings in the image
Consistency between AI annotations and prior case experience

probes.question: When reviewing an image with AI annotations, do you rely more on a fixed scanning path (e.g., top-left to bottom-right) or on jumping directly to annotated regions?
Does disruption of your visual search pattern increase more when there are many overlapping annotations or when background image noise is high?
Which aspect of AI annotations most disrupts your natural eye movement: high-contrast bounding boxes or large, brightly colored overlays?

probes.attribute: Spatial proximity of AI cues to regions of interest
probes.question: Is your search pattern more disrupted when AI annotations are near known anatomical landmarks or when they appear in unexpected locations?

probes.attribute: Temporal sequence of annotation appearance during review
probes.question: Does your visual search get disrupted more when all annotations appear at once or when they appear sequentially as you scroll?
choices: Annotations appear sequentially as you scroll

probes.attribute: Expert’s familiarity with the image type or modality
probes.question: Is disruption of your visual search pattern worse when reviewing unfamiliar modalities (e.g., new imaging technique) or familiar ones with misleading AI cues?
choices: Familiar modalities with misleading AI cues

probes.attribute: Density of true-positive findings in the image
probes.question: Does a high density of actual findings make AI-induced disruption more tolerable or more confusing?

probes.attribute: Consistency between AI annotations and prior case experience
probes.question: Is your visual search more disrupted when AI annotations contradict your past experience or when they highlight novel but plausible findings?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If annotation salience changed from subtle gray outlines to flashing red boxes, would your judgment about 'Disruption of visual search pattern' change?
If spatial proximity of AI cues shifted from centered on known lesions to randomly scattered across the image, would your judgment about 'Disruption of visual search pattern' change?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Spatial proximity of AI cues to regions of interest

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If your familiarity with the image modality changed from routine chest X-rays to a novel contrast-enhanced MRI protocol, would your judgment about 'Disruption of visual search pattern' change?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Expert’s familiarity with the image type or modality

aligned_path_expert: Lack of DICOM-compliant output from AI vendors
aligned_path_researcher: Identify key problems AI should solve

conflict.expert_branch: Lack of DICOM-compliant output from AI vendors
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify key problems AI should solve

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring AI outputs are DICOM-compliant is essential—it directly enables seamless integration into existing clinical imaging workflows and ensures diagnostic results are accessible where radiologists already work.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on technical interoperability may result in AI tools that integrate smoothly but don’t address the most impactful clinical or operational problems, leading to underutilization despite compliance.
pivot.research_goal: Identify key problems AI should solve—such as reducing redundant scans, accelerating differential diagnosis, or minimizing unnecessary follow-ups—by grounding development in real clinical needs.
pivot.compelling_reason: By aligning AI capabilities with high-value clinical challenges from the outset, you ensure that compliant tools aren’t just technically usable but actively sought after because they meaningfully improve diagnostic speed, accuracy, or patient outcomes.
pivot.coarse_scenario: A radiologist receives an AI-enhanced study that not only appears natively in PACS but also flags a subtle finding linked to an early-stage condition the team had nearly missed, prompting timely intervention. Because the AI was designed around a problem clinicians actually face—detecting low-prevalence anomalies in routine scans—it becomes a trusted daily partner, not just another compliant widget.

aligned_path_expert: Inability to edit or override AI markup
aligned_path_researcher: Determine needs for result exploration and filtering

conflict.expert_branch: Inability to edit or override AI markup
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine needs for result exploration and filtering

analogy.source_concept: Lack of DICOM-compliant output from AI vendors

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated annotations or results that need to be stored and accessed within standard clinical systems
structural_mapping.logic: The system must translate or embed results into a format that integrates with existing infrastructure without disrupting workflow
structural_mapping.outputs: Annotations or findings that are persistently available, queryable, and editable within the viewer or archive

analogy.explanation: Just as non-DICOM-compliant AI outputs become 'orphaned' data that can't be managed within PACS—forcing radiologists to work around them—the researcher’s need for result exploration and filtering reflects a parallel requirement: AI outputs must be structured so users can interrogate, refine, and contextualize them. In both cases, if the system doesn’t expose results in a manipulable, standards-aligned way, the user loses control over interpretation.

scenario.inputs: A chest CT scan where an AI tool highlights three lung nodules, but one is a false positive caused by a vessel crossing artifact—a common error radiologists recognize instantly
scenario.outputs: Instead of just toggling the AI layer on/off, the researcher’s approach would allow the radiologist to filter out annotations below a certain confidence threshold, sort by anatomical region, or drill into why the AI flagged each nodule (e.g., showing feature attributions). This enables correction through informed refinement rather than blunt override.
scenario.edge_cases: When the AI markup is embedded as a static overlay with no metadata (e.g., no confidence score, location tags, or modifiable state), the researcher’s filtering/exploration features become impossible to implement—highlighting that editability and explorability both depend on underlying data structure, not just UI controls.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify pain points in current workflows

conflict.expert_branch: Latency during peak reading hours
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify pain points in current workflows

attributes: Number of concurrent studies in queue
Prioritization rules for urgent vs. routine cases

probes.question: Is your concern about latency during peak reading hours more tied to visual interface clutter or to unexpected case interruptions?
Does high ambient noise (>80dB) affect your perception of system latency more than it affects your ability to focus on image interpretation?
choices: Affects perception of system latency more

probes.attribute: Number of concurrent studies in queue
probes.question: Which matters more for perceived AI slowdown: the absolute number of studies queued or the rate at which new studies arrive?
choices: Rate at which new studies arrive

probes.question: Is latency during peak reading hours worse between 8–10 AM or 2–4 PM, based on your experience?
Does perceived AI latency increase more when you’re fatigued or when the system is actually under heavy load?
choices: When the system is under heavy load

probes.question: Which contributes more to processing delays: the number of series per study or the number of custom layout rules in the hanging protocol?
Is AI latency more sensitive to CPU bottlenecks or GPU memory saturation during peak hours?

probes.attribute: Prioritization rules for urgent vs. routine cases
probes.question: Does reordering the queue for STAT cases increase perceived latency for routine studies more than it improves turnaround for urgent ones?
choices: Increases latency for routine studies more
Improves turnaround for urgent cases more

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the number of concurrent studies in the queue dropped from 25 to 5 during typical morning peak hours, would your judgment about 'Latency during peak reading hours' significantly improve?
hypothetical_scenarios.attribute: Number of concurrent studies in queue

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If ambient noise in the reading room were reduced from 85dB to 55dB while all other conditions remained the same, would your perception of AI-induced latency during peak hours change?
If hanging protocols were simplified to use only default layouts instead of custom configurations, would you notice a meaningful reduction in AI processing delays during high-volume periods?

aligned_path_expert: No clear audit trail for AI-assisted reads
aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: No clear audit trail for AI-assisted reads
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring clear audit trails for AI-assisted reads is essential for legal defensibility and clinical accountability—without it, institutions risk liability exposure and eroded trust in diagnostic workflows.

pivot.limitation: Even with a perfect audit trail, if radiologists don’t reliably understand when to trust or question the AI’s output based on their domain knowledge, the recorded decisions may still be flawed or inconsistent, undermining the very accountability the trail is meant to support.
pivot.research_goal: Understand how domain knowledge shapes experts’ reliability judgments of AI outputs
pivot.compelling_reason: By clarifying how clinical expertise informs trust in AI, we can design audit systems that not only log who did what, but also capture why decisions were made—strengthening both legal defensibility and diagnostic accuracy.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical morning read session, a radiologist reviews an AI-highlighted lung nodule. Because the system transparently surfaces how the finding aligns—or conflicts—with known disease patterns, the radiologist confidently overrides a false positive, and the audit trail now includes not just the action, but the clinically grounded rationale. Later, during a peer review or legal inquiry, the decision is easily validated as both expert-driven and well-reasoned.

aligned_path_expert: Pressure to accept AI suggestions to meet productivity targets
aligned_path_researcher: Identify key problems AI should solve

conflict.expert_branch: Pressure to accept AI suggestions to meet productivity targets
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify key problems AI should solve

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the pressure clinicians face to accept AI suggestions due to productivity targets is critically important—it reflects real-world constraints where efficiency metrics can override careful clinical judgment.

pivot.limitation: Relying on AI outputs primarily to meet productivity quotas risks embedding tools that don’t actually solve the right clinical problems, potentially increasing diagnostic errors or clinician burnout over time.
pivot.research_goal: Identify key problems AI should solve
pivot.compelling_reason: By first clarifying which specific diagnostic or operational challenges AI must address—such as reducing false positives or streamlining differential diagnosis—you ensure that the tools adopted genuinely support clinical accuracy and reduce cognitive load, rather than just creating the illusion of efficiency.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical day, clinicians interact with an AI system that anticipates their most pressing diagnostic uncertainties and offers targeted, explainable insights—freeing them to focus on complex patient discussions instead of chasing arbitrary throughput metrics. The result is fewer rushed decisions, higher diagnostic confidence, and a workflow that aligns productivity with patient outcomes.

aligned_path_expert: Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators
aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

probes.question: Is your concern more about balancing patient safety against administrative efficiency, or about preserving professional autonomy versus adopting AI recommendations?
choices: Balancing patient safety vs. administrative efficiency
Preserving professional autonomy vs. adopting AI recommendations

probes.question: Does your concern stem more from AI models performing poorly on rare conditions due to class imbalance, or from administrators not recognizing that imbalance affects reliability?
choices: AI models performing poorly on rare conditions due to class imbalance
Administrators not recognizing that imbalance affects reliability

probes.question: Which matters more in your fear of being second-guessed: the potential harm from a missed diagnosis, or the likelihood that administrators will ignore clinical context when AI disagrees with you?
choices: Potential harm from a missed diagnosis
Administrators ignoring clinical context when AI disagrees

probes.question: Is your concern driven more by administrators lacking technical understanding of AI limitations, or by their overconfidence in algorithmic outputs regardless of context?
choices: Administrators lacking technical understanding of AI limitations
Administrators' overconfidence in algorithmic outputs regardless of context

probes.question: Which increases your fear more: using a black-box AI model with no explainability, or using a transparent model that administrators still treat as infallible?
choices: Using a black-box AI model with no explainability
Using a transparent model that administrators still treat as infallible

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to how often you encounter ambiguous or atypical imaging findings, or to how consistently AI fails on those edge cases?
choices: How often you encounter ambiguous or atypical imaging findings
How consistently AI fails on those edge cases

probes.question: Does your fear increase more when there’s no formal process to appeal AI-influenced decisions, or when appeals are possible but rarely succeed?
choices: No formal process to appeal AI-influenced decisions
Appeals are possible but rarely succeed

probes.question: Is your concern stronger when administrators have previously overruled radiologists based on AI, or when they’ve never done so but show signs of preparing to?
choices: Administrators have previously overruled radiologists based on AI
Administrators have never done so but show signs of preparing to

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the hospital implemented mandatory AI literacy training for all non-radiologist administrators, would your judgment about 'Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators' change?

If the AI system were modified to flag only high-prevalence, low-severity findings (e.g., common benign nodules) instead of rare, high-severity conditions, would your judgment about 'Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators' change?

If your institution adopted a formal review board where radiologists could contest AI-influenced administrative decisions with full clinical context, would your judgment about 'Fear of being second-guessed by non-radiologist administrators' change?

conflict.expert_branch: Patients assume AI = infallible
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess impact of uncertainty communication

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on managing patient expectations around AI infallibility is crucial—it directly protects clinical integrity and ensures informed consent remains meaningful in real-world care settings.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on general disclaimers or verbal reassurances may not effectively recalibrate patient trust, especially when patients lack context to interpret AI limitations.
pivot.research_goal: Assess how communicating prediction uncertainty—such as confidence levels or alternative diagnoses—affects patient perceptions of reliability and decision-making.
pivot.compelling_reason: By grounding communication in evidence-based uncertainty framing, you gain a clinically actionable tool that actively shapes realistic expectations, reduces liability risk, and strengthens shared decision-making.
pivot.coarse_scenario: During a routine consultation, a clinician presents an AI-generated diagnosis alongside a simple visual cue indicating moderate confidence. The patient, now understanding the result isn’t absolute, asks clarifying questions and collaborates more openly on next steps—leading to a more informed and trusting interaction.

aligned_path_researcher: Determine format preferences for supporting evidence

conflict.expert_branch: Lack of standardized disclosure language
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine format preferences for supporting evidence

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on establishing standardized disclosure language is essential—it directly addresses the ethical and legal imperative to ensure patients understand when AI contributes to their diagnosis, fostering trust and informed consent in clinical practice.

pivot.limitation: Even with clear disclosure language, clinicians may struggle to justify or explain AI-informed decisions if they lack intuitive access to the underlying evidence in a format that aligns with their cognitive workflow.
pivot.research_goal: Determine format preferences for supporting evidence—such as interactive knowledge graphs or summary panels—that best enable clinicians to comprehend and communicate the rationale behind AI-assisted diagnoses.
pivot.compelling_reason: By aligning evidence presentation with clinician preferences, you not only strengthen the credibility of your disclosure but also empower providers to confidently answer patient questions, reducing liability risk and enhancing shared decision-making.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, a physician reviews an AI-assisted diagnosis and instantly accesses a concise, visually intuitive summary of supporting evidence tailored to their preference. During the patient visit, they seamlessly reference this information to explain the AI’s role, reinforcing transparency and building trust without disrupting clinical flow.

aligned_path_expert: Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians
aligned_path_researcher: Evaluate utility of XAI techniques for perturbation understanding

conflict.expert_branch: Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate utility of XAI techniques for perturbation understanding

probes.question: When overriding AI, do you rely more on rule-based shortcuts (e.g., 'always trust motion artifact over AI') or case-specific synthesis?
Which matters more when justifying your override to a clinician: that your reasoning is consistent across similar cases, or that it yields the highest diagnostic accuracy?
Is your difficulty in justifying disagreement greater when the AI ignores patient history, or when it misinterprets imaging findings alone?
Do you find it harder to justify overriding AI when the discrepancy involves a subtle change from prior scans, or when it’s a finding absent in priors?
When justifying a disagreement, does your emphasis shift more toward avoiding false negatives or avoiding false positives?
Is it harder to justify your override to a specialist (e.g., neurologist) or to a generalist (e.g., primary care physician)?
Does having a documented prior case with similar AI disagreement make justification easier or harder?
Is your hesitation to justify AI disagreement more about violating departmental consensus norms or about sounding personally defensive?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the referring clinician were a radiologist instead of a non-radiologist specialist, would your judgment about 'Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians' change?
If the AI’s output included a clear confidence score that was low (<30%), would your judgment about 'Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians' change?

If hospital policy formally recognized radiologist overrides as standard practice without requiring detailed justification, would your judgment about 'Difficulty justifying AI disagreement to referring clinicians' change?

aligned_path_expert: Trainees skip systematic review when AI is active
aligned_path_researcher: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction

conflict.expert_branch: Trainees skip systematic review when AI is active
conflict.researcher_branch: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on trainees skipping systematic review when AI is active rightly highlights a critical risk to clinical skill development and long-term diagnostic competence.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on preventing AI overreliance may miss opportunities to actively shape how trainees interact with AI to reinforce—not replace—their foundational reasoning.
pivot.research_goal: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction
pivot.compelling_reason: By intentionally structuring AI interactions around clear knowledge objectives—like distinguishing signal from noise or tracing causal pathways—trainees can use AI as a scaffold for deeper inquiry rather than a shortcut, preserving autonomy while accelerating insight.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical morning case review, a resident engages with an AI tool not just to get answers, but to explore which findings matter most and why. Guided by prioritized knowledge goals, they practice disciplined questioning, turning each AI suggestion into a learning moment that strengthens their diagnostic judgment.

aligned_path_expert: Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition
aligned_path_researcher: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction

conflict.expert_branch: Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition
conflict.researcher_branch: Prioritize knowledge goals from model interaction

probes.question: Is your reduced confidence more tied to the quality of input data (e.g., imaging resolution, lab precision) or the structure of the data (e.g., missing modalities, inconsistent formats)?
Which matters more for your diagnostic hesitation: the absolute rarity of the disease or the relative imbalance between common and rare conditions in your daily caseload?
Does your confidence drop more sharply after 6 months without seeing a rare case or after 2 years?
Which threshold better predicts your confidence: having seen fewer than 3 cases ever, or fewer than 1 case in the last year?
choices: Fewer than 1 case in the last year

probes.question: When uncertain, do you tend to overestimate your diagnostic accuracy or underestimate it for rare diseases?
Is your reduced confidence more affected by early misclassification due to common mimics or by lack of confirmatory testing?
choices: Early misclassification due to common mimics

probes.question: Which delays hurt your learning more: slow pathology confirmation or delayed patient follow-up?
Does your confidence suffer more when your institution sees only one subtype of a rare disease or when you never see any cases at all?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If your clinical exposure frequency increased from once every 3 years to once every 6 months for a specific rare disease, would your judgment about 'Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition' improve significantly?

If feedback latency for rare disease diagnoses was reduced from 6 months to 2 weeks through rapid genomic confirmation, would your judgment about 'Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition' change?

If AI tools presented synthetic but realistic rare disease cases monthly during training, preserving pattern recognition despite low real-world exposure, would your judgment about 'Reduced confidence in rare disease recognition' change?

aligned_path_expert: Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence
aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

probes.question: When deciding whether to override an AI finding, which matters more: minimizing patient harm or avoiding unnecessary follow-up procedures?
Is your hesitation to contradict AI more influenced by the clinical setting (e.g., ER vs. screening clinic) or by the type of imaging modality used?
Does your willingness to challenge AI output decrease more under high caseload volume or tight reporting deadlines?
Which affects your trust more: knowing the AI’s false positive rate or its performance on cases similar to the current one?
When evaluating AI disagreement, do you weigh resemblance to your own past cases more heavily than published validation studies?
choices: Resemblance to your own past cases

probes.question: Is your hesitation stronger when your institution audits AI overrides or when peer review is anonymous?
Does a heatmap highlighting the AI’s region of interest reduce your hesitation more than a confidence score alone?
Has your tendency to second-guess yourself increased more after missing an AI-corrected finding or after falsely overriding a correct AI call?
choices: Falsely overriding a correct AI call

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If time pressure intensity changed from routine screening workload to trauma triage during a mass casualty event, would your judgment about 'Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence' change?

If perceived AI explainability changed from a black-box output to a saliency map aligned with known anatomical landmarks, would your judgment about 'Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence' change?

If institutional accountability norms changed from no formal review of AI overrides to mandatory multidisciplinary justification for every override, would your judgment about 'Hesitation to contradict AI without strong evidence' change?

conflict.expert_branch: Oversimplification of borderline cases
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify desired granularity of explanations

analogy.source_concept: Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately

structural_mapping.inputs: A borderline imaging finding (e.g., 45% carotid stenosis) with ambiguous clinical significance
structural_mapping.logic: Forcing a probabilistic or continuous assessment into a binary or categorical output without expressing the underlying spectrum of plausible interpretations
structural_mapping.outputs: A definitive label (e.g., 'moderate stenosis') that masks the inherent uncertainty and nuance

analogy.explanation: Just as AI systems often suppress diagnostic uncertainty by delivering point estimates instead of confidence intervals or alternative interpretations, the researcher’s focus on explanation granularity addresses how much detail is needed to faithfully represent the reasoning behind a decision. In both cases, the core issue is whether the system preserves or discards gradational information—whether in diagnosis (expert concern) or in causal explanation (researcher concern). The researcher’s question about desired granularity mirrors the expert’s worry: too coarse an explanation (like too coarse a diagnosis) erases critical nuance.

scenario.inputs: A chest CT scan showing a 7mm pulmonary nodule with indistinct margins in a 62-year-old former smoker; clinical history includes rheumatoid arthritis (which can cause benign nodules)
scenario.outputs: The AI provides not just a malignancy probability but also layered explanations: at the finest granularity, it highlights specific radiomic features (e.g., spiculation score); at a coarser level, it references population-level risk pathways (e.g., 'nodule size + smoking history → elevated risk'); the researcher seeks to determine which layer clinicians find most actionable for borderline cases like this one
scenario.edge_cases: When the nodule’s features straddle benign and malignant categories, the expert expects the AI to express uncertainty and integrate clinical context (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis), whereas the researcher’s granularity framework might prioritize mechanistic explanations (e.g., feature importance) over contextual integration—revealing a misalignment between explanation depth and diagnostic reasoning needs

aligned_path_expert: High false positive rate in lung nodule detection
aligned_path_researcher: Assess familiarity and prior experience with AI gene models

conflict.expert_branch: High false positive rate in lung nodule detection
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess familiarity and prior experience with AI gene models

structural_mapping.inputs: Ambiguous radiological findings (e.g., indeterminate opacities) without sufficient clinical context
structural_mapping.logic: AI assigns high diagnostic confidence based solely on visual pattern matching, ignoring contextual uncertainty
structural_mapping.outputs: High-confidence alerts that prompt unnecessary interventions or anxiety

analogy.explanation: Just as AI overconfidently labels ambiguous lung findings as definitive nodules without considering clinical nuance, researchers assessing familiarity with AI gene models must recognize that users may overestimate their understanding or the tool's reliability based on superficial exposure—leading to misplaced trust or rejection. Both scenarios involve a mismatch between system confidence and real-world interpretive complexity.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist using an AI lung nodule detector flags a small perifissural opacity as malignant; simultaneously, the same radiologist is asked to complete a survey about their prior use of AI tools like DeepVariant or AlphaMissense for variant calling
scenario.outputs: The researcher compiles usage frequency, self-rated proficiency, and past reliance on AI gene predictions to infer whether the radiologist would adopt a new AI diagnostic aid—and how they might interpret its outputs
scenario.edge_cases: The radiologist has never used genomic AI tools but frequently uses imaging AI; they conflate confidence in imaging AI (despite high false positives) with expectations for genomic AI, leading the researcher to misattribute adoption readiness based on unrelated domain experience

aligned_path_expert: Missed subtle fractures on trauma X-rays
aligned_path_researcher: Evaluate utility of XAI techniques for perturbation understanding

conflict.expert_branch: Missed subtle fractures on trauma X-rays
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate utility of XAI techniques for perturbation understanding

structural_mapping.inputs: An X-ray image with indeterminate or borderline features that lack definitive fracture signs (e.g., faint cortical disruption without clear displacement)
structural_mapping.logic: The AI interprets subtle visual cues as definitive evidence and assigns high diagnostic confidence without accounting for contextual uncertainty or the need for clinical correlation
structural_mapping.outputs: A high-confidence prediction of 'no fracture' despite the presence of a hairline fracture that a radiologist would flag as suspicious based on surrounding anatomical context

analogy.explanation: Just as overconfidence in ambiguous findings reflects an AI’s failure to calibrate its certainty to the true diagnostic ambiguity, evaluating XAI techniques for perturbation understanding seeks to reveal how small, realistic changes to an input (like slight rotations or contrast shifts mimicking real-world imaging variability) alter the model’s confidence. In both cases, the core issue is the model’s inability to reflect appropriate uncertainty—XAI methods aim to expose this by showing which perturbations flip the model’s decision, thereby mirroring the expert’s concern about unwarranted certainty in borderline trauma cases.

scenario.inputs: A wrist X-ray from a trauma patient showing a non-displaced scaphoid fracture: the cortical line is subtly interrupted but without angulation or visible gap; soft tissue swelling is present but minimal.
scenario.outputs: An XAI technique (e.g., integrated gradients) applied to the AI model highlights regions of the image that most influenced the 'no fracture' prediction. When the image is perturbed—such as by adding simulated motion blur or adjusting windowing—the explanation changes dramatically, revealing that the model relies heavily on background texture rather than bone contour continuity, thus exposing fragility in its reasoning.
scenario.edge_cases: When the fracture is so subtle that even expert radiologists disagree on its presence (inter-observer variability), the researcher’s XAI evaluation may still produce a confident saliency map, falsely implying robustness, while the expert would expect the system to signal uncertainty or request human review—highlighting a divergence in how 'acceptable performance' is defined.

aligned_path_expert: Poor performance on pediatric or geriatric anatomy
aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Poor performance on pediatric or geriatric anatomy
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

structural_mapping.inputs: Medical images acquired under different scanner protocols (e.g., Siemens vs. GE), each introducing subtle but systematic differences in contrast, resolution, or noise patterns
structural_mapping.logic: The AI model applies learned features from its training distribution without recognizing that vendor-specific acquisition parameters alter the appearance of normal anatomy, leading to misclassification of normal variants as pathology
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous segmentations or diagnoses that reflect scanner-specific artifacts rather than true biological variation

analogy.explanation: Just as vendor differences introduce imaging variations that fool models trained on a narrow set of protocols, age-related anatomical changes (e.g., thinner cortical bone in geriatrics or incomplete ossification in pediatrics) represent another form of ‘distribution shift’—not due to equipment, but biology. In both cases, the model lacks embedded knowledge of what constitutes expected variation, and experts must rely on their domain understanding to judge whether an output is plausible. The researcher’s focus on how domain knowledge informs reliability judgments mirrors how radiologists already discount AI outputs when they recognize vendor-induced artifacts, applying similar skepticism when outputs contradict known age-specific norms.

scenario.inputs: A brain MRI from a 3-year-old child showing large ventricles and thin white matter—normal for age—but processed by an AI model trained exclusively on adults aged 30–60
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a structured assessment showing that radiologists reject the AI’s ‘ventriculomegaly’ flag not because the algorithm is technically flawed, but because their biological knowledge of neurodevelopment overrides the model’s output; this judgment is then used to refine reliability criteria that weight expert ontological priors
scenario.edge_cases: When the pediatric scan also includes a subtle pathology (e.g., early leukodystrophy) that mimics normal developmental appearance, the expert’s domain knowledge may lead them to dismiss a correct AI alert, revealing tension between justified skepticism and missed detection—highlighting where reliability judgments based on domain knowledge can themselves be fallible

aligned_path_researcher: Identify key problems AI should solve

conflict.expert_branch: Inconsistent results across MRI vendors
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify key problems AI should solve

structural_mapping.inputs: MRI scans from older or portable scanners with lower signal-to-noise ratios and non-standard protocols, analogous to the variability introduced by different vendor hardware/software
structural_mapping.logic: AI model trained on high-quality, standardized data fails to generalize when input characteristics deviate from training distribution—whether due to scanner age or vendor-specific acquisition parameters
structural_mapping.outputs: Segmentation or diagnostic outputs that are inconsistent, noisy, or clinically unreliable, just as they would be across Siemens vs. GE vs. Philips systems

analogy.explanation: Just as AI tools break down when applied to low-resource imaging because they weren’t trained on the full spectrum of real-world equipment variability, they similarly fail across MRI vendors due to unaccounted-for protocol differences. In both cases, the core issue is a mismatch between training data diversity and deployment conditions. The researcher’s goal—to identify which problems AI should solve—aligns with prioritizing generalization challenges like these as key targets for development.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist uploads a T1-weighted brain MRI acquired on a Philips Ingenia 3T scanner using a non-standard reconstruction kernel to an AI segmentation tool trained primarily on Siemens Prisma data with vendor-default settings.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a structured problem statement such as: 'AI must reliably segment white matter hyperintensities across vendor-specific acquisition protocols without manual recalibration,' which then guides dataset curation, augmentation strategies, or domain-adaptation methods.
scenario.edge_cases: When the same AI tool performs adequately on a GE scanner but fails on Philips—even with identical anatomical pathology—the expert sees a technical inconsistency, while the researcher interprets it as evidence that 'cross-vendor robustness' is a critical problem AI must solve, not just a nuisance to workaround.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify key problems AI should solve

conflict.expert_branch: AI ignores clinical history integration
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify key problems AI should solve

analogy.source_concept: Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately

structural_mapping.inputs: Clinical data fed into AI without contextual qualifiers (e.g., patient history, comorbidities)
structural_mapping.logic: AI processes inputs as fixed signals rather than probabilistic or conditional evidence, just as it suppresses uncertainty in its outputs
structural_mapping.outputs: A definitive recommendation or classification that lacks nuance about what the system doesn’t know or how context might shift interpretation

analogy.explanation: Just as AI fails to express diagnostic uncertainty—offering a single answer when multiple interpretations are plausible—it also fails to identify which problems are worth solving because it doesn’t incorporate the clinician’s contextual priorities. The researcher’s goal of 'identifying key problems AI should solve' requires the same kind of contextual awareness that underlies expressing uncertainty: understanding what is unknown, what is ambiguous, and what hinges on patient-specific factors like clinical history. Both stem from AI treating inputs as static facts rather than situated evidence.

scenario.inputs: A chest X-ray from a 68-year-old patient with recent lung resection surgery and ongoing immunosuppressive therapy for rheumatoid arthritis, showing a new opacity
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a prioritized list of AI functionalities clinicians need—such as tools that flag post-surgical changes versus infection by integrating medication history and procedure dates—based on interviews where experts describe this exact scenario as a recurring diagnostic dilemma
scenario.edge_cases: If the AI tool is trained only on radiographic features without access to EHR data, it may classify the opacity as 'likely pneumonia,' while the expert knows immunosuppression and recent surgery make fungal infection or atelectasis more probable. The researcher’s problem-identification process would surface this gap as a key use case, but only if the elicitation method explicitly probes for failures tied to missing clinical history—not just image-level performance.

aligned_path_expert: Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately

conflict.expert_branch: Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately
conflict.researcher_branch: Surface concerns about model limitations

probes.question: When evaluating AI output, do you rely more on rule-based shortcuts (e.g., 'if biomarker X is elevated, suspect Y') or on integrating multiple probabilistic signals?
Which matters more to you in clinical practice: consistent predictions that align with your expectations, or technically accurate outputs that may be surprising or counterintuitive?
choices: Technically accurate but potentially surprising outputs

probes.question: Is your concern about AI output more related to overconfident point estimates or to the absence of calibrated uncertainty ranges (e.g., 95% prediction intervals)?
Do you find AI systems fail most often in cases just below or just above your personal threshold for diagnostic ambiguity?
When assessing diagnostic uncertainty, do you weigh false negatives more heavily than false positives, or vice versa?
Does your discomfort with AI uncertainty increase more when the condition is rare in your patient population or when it’s common but variable in presentation?
Is your concern more about AI providing a single rigid interpretation or about its inability to suggest alternative plausible diagnoses?
choices: Inability to suggest alternative plausible diagnoses

probes.question: Do you worry more about AI missing evolving diagnostic trajectories or about misclassifying stable conditions as uncertain?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI system began providing 90% prediction intervals alongside its point estimate, would your judgment about 'Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately' improve significantly?

If the same AI output were applied to a pediatric population instead of adults, would your concern about 'Inability to convey diagnostic uncertainty appropriately' increase due to lower prior prevalence of certain conditions?
If the AI suggested two equally plausible alternative diagnoses with associated likelihoods instead of one point estimate, would your trust in its handling of uncertainty increase?
